data.title: Walmart, Home Depot Set To Report Earnings
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

The stock market opened in the red on Thursday after the wholesale inflation came in much hotter than expected. The U.S. Producer Price Index rose 0.9% M/M in July, surpassing the 0.2% increase expected and June's unrevised flat reading. On a year-over-year basis, wholesale inflation accelerated to +3.3%, well above the +2.5% consensus. U.S. stocks advanced to mark record closes on Wednesday as a rally sparked by heightened expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts continued.

Amid increased expectations of a Fed rate cut in September, investors will keep a close eye on economic reports due to be announced next week. These include the NAHB Housing Market Index on Monday, Building Permits and Housing Starts on Tuesday, and MBA Mortgage Applications, Crude Oil Imports, Gasoline Production, and FOMC Meeting Minutes on Wednesday. Thursday will include reports on Initial Jobless Claims, and Existing Home Sales, with the Baker Hughes Rig Count report due on Friday.

Walmart (WMT), Intuit (INTU), Workday (WDAY), Dollar Tree (DLTR), Zoom Communications (ZM), Palo Alto Networks (PANW), Fabrinet (FN), Home Depot (HD), Lowe's (LOW), Analog Devices (ADI), Target (TGT), and Baidu (BIDU) will report earnings next week.

_____________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, August 18: Palo Alto Networks, Fabrinet, Agora (API), and Blink Charging (BLNK). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, August 19: Home Depot, Medtronic (MDT), and Keysight Technologies (KEYS). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, August 20: Lowe's, Analog Devices, Target, and Baidu. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, August 21: Walmart, Intuit, Workday, Dollar Tree, and Zoom Communications. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, August 22: Buckle (BKE), and Prosafe (OTCPK:PRSEF). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Opendoor Technologies (OPEN) and Tilray (TLRY) set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Celcuity (CELC), Rigel Pharma (RIGL), and Indivior (INDV). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Spirit Aviation (FLYY), Vtex (VTEX), and Monday.com (MNDY).

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include Constellation Energy (CEG), Carlyle (CG), Cognizant (CTSH), KLA (KLAC), Papa John's International (PZZA), Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN), Wynn Resorts (WYNN), Thomson Reuters (TRI), Hasbro (HAS), Warner Music (WMG), Applied Materials (AMAT), Marriott International (MAR), Microsoft (MSFT), Amgen (AMGN). Companies forecast to increase their quarterly dividends include Intuit (INTU), from $1.04 to $1.18, and Altria (MO), from $1.02 to $1.04. Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

IPO Watch: Elite Express (ETS) is expected to price its IPO and begin trading. The company, which through its unit provides last-mile delivery services within California, is looking to raise $16M. The IPO lockup period expires for blocks of shares of Anbio Biotechnology (NNNN).

Walmart Earnings Preview: Retail giant Walmart is scheduled to report second quarter earnings next week, with analysts expecting earnings per share of $0.74 on a revenue of $174.21B. Following a more difficult backdrop to start this year, due to unexpected tariff and expense headwinds, analysts believe a positive guidance revision cycle could again materialize soon. Oppenheimer analysts have lifted their Q2 constant currency sales projection to reflect the potential for stronger top-line momentum.

Home Depot Earnings Preview: Home Depot is set to report its second quarter earnings next week and is expected to report earnings per share of $4.70 on a revenue of $45.34B. The broader macro backdrop for home improvement remains challenged but directionally improving as we move through mid-2025. While transaction trends and weather conditions have stabilized versus earlier this year, the consumer remains cautious, with affordability pressures, inflation fatigue, and macro uncertainty weighing on larger project demand.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VanEck Gold Miners ETF: Leveraged Play On Gold In An Uncertain World
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome High Alpha Professor as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

President Trump recently said he would not tariff gold bars. Surprisingly, these positive words hurt gold stocks, which had been rallying while there were fears of a high US tariff on bullions. As the just cited Seeking Alpha article noted, following this news, “GDX was 0.78% down to $57.61 during afternoon trading after adding 11.91% over the course of the last six trading days.”

Unlike some investments, gold loses its shine on positive macroeconomic developments. However, gold is a safe haven during uncertain economic conditions. Despite the President’s latest positive words on gold, the economy, in general, is not doing so well. Therefore, the quick decline presents an opportunity.

I have always been intrigued by physical gold. In Dubai, where I work, we have the gold souk - a wonderful world full of golden colors. Despite that allure, there are a number of reasons why gold etfs may be better as investments than physical gold, while gold miner stocks may be better than etfs. So the progression is - gold miners > gold etfs > physical gold.

Among miners, I like VanEck Gold Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:GDX) because it offers investors exposure to global gold mining companies. These days, global macroeconomics is defined by political uncertainty, de-dollarization efforts from BRICS countries, and broad geopolitical stability. These things have taken gold prices to record highs. Gold miners have some operational leverage over gold ETFs, which has helped them significantly outperform those ETFs. My outlook here is medium term - say, 3-4 years - and I will look at what factors could help GDX do even better than it is doing now and what are the risks to a bullish thesis.

Investment Thesis

In working out my investment thesis, I consider five factors. The first factor is the need for a safe haven under current political uncertainties. This demand pushed gold to all-time highs above $3,400/oz in 2025. I think this trend will continue for a while longer. The second factor is the strong performance of gold miner stocks, which have outdone physical gold year to date. As of this month, GDX is up ~71% YTD, far exceeding the ~25–30% gains in bullion-tracking ETFs. Over the past year, GDX returned ~64% vs ~40% for gold funds, which again shows the leveraged upside of mining stocks in a gold bull market. The third factor is record mining margins. Gold miners are seeing historically record profit margins. As one source says - “With gold averaging $2,866 per ounce in Q1 2025 (up 38.3% year-over-year) and all-in sustaining costs (AISC) projected around $1,525 per ounce, gold miners are experiencing profit margins unprecedented in the industry's history.”

Next, the fourth factor is the operational leverage inherent in gold mining. Because miners have largely fixed costs, even a small rise in gold prices can cause a much larger percentage increase in their margins and earnings. This profit amplification often makes mining stocks — and by extension GDX — move 2–3 times as much as the underlying gold price in either direction. Lastly, the fifth factor, is the consideration that the flipside of a miner’s leverage is higher volatility and risk. GDX has historically seen deeper drawdowns (peak declines >80%) than gold’s ~45% drops. When gold goes down, mining stocks may go down disproportionately. GDX investors should remember this. They should be able to tolerate these swings and look at the higher return potential, especially in a bad market. The idea is to get in when the market is bad, get out when it starts recovering. That is why I said my investment window is mid-term.

Macro Drivers

There are a number of macro drivers you have to consider before investing in GDX. Broadly, these are: one, political uncertainty and de-dollarization trends, two, geopolitical instability, and three, inflation and related monetary policies.

Political uncertainty in the US has undermined global confidence in US assets, driving investors towards gold. The dollar’s stability has been called into question by rising deficits and debt. As of Q1 2025, U.S. total public debt-to-GDP stood at approximately 120%, reflecting a sustained elevated debt burden. Budget deficit grew to $291 billion in July despite a surge in tariff revenue. These figures raise the specter of fiscal weakness and heighten macroeconomic uncertainty in the US.

This regional uncertainty has, in turn, caused global uncertainty. A number of central banks of various countries have started buying gold in an effort to move away from the dollar. They have been buying up gold at a record pace. Poland led the tide, with its central bank buying ~90 tonnes of gold last year, followed by Turkey at ~75 tonnes and India at ~72 tonnes. A whopping 1086 tonnes of gold was bought in this way in 2024, with another ~1000 tonnes expected to be bought this year. This is a strong tailwind to gold prices. As Reuters notes, “the drivers that have underpinned de-dollarisation in recent years remain firmly in place,” with many countries losing confidence in U.S. Treasuries and finding gold as the ultimate safe-haven. All this benefits GDX’ underlying holdings.

Geopolitical instability surrounding the Ukraine war, the Gaza war, the brief India-Pakistan war have all contributed to gold’s resurgence as a safe haven. For example, a month after the Ukraine war started, gold prices rose approximately 3%. This so-called “fear trade” benefits miners like GDX. However, if there is any easing of risks, that will reduce interest in gold, and miners will suffer more than gold. Since these risks may not outlast the current political scenario, I have a medium-term, 3-4 year thesis on GDX.

There was a huge spike in inflation during the pandemic, with US CPI surging to 9% in 2022. These have now fallen back, standing at a modest 2.7% today, a fraction of the peak. The Fed has therefore stopped hiking rates, and while easing has not been a lot - 100 bps in late 2024 only - there could be further reduction in interest rates as soon as the September FOMC meeting. If that happens, it could be a strong tailwind for gold, setting the stage for a supportive monetary backdrop.

With inflation now near the Fed’s target, focus has turned to interest rates — and falling rates tend to favor gold. Because gold doesn’t pay interest, lower yields reduce the opportunity cost of holding it, making the metal more attractive to investors. Last year, the European Central Bank aggressively cut rates (eight times), creating a globally easier monetary backdrop. This helped gold gain 30%. If Feds cut rates, this may weaken the dollar and strengthen gold.

Source - Fed Funds Rate data from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (FRED), gold price data from the World Gold Council and LBMA.

This chart makes it clear that gold has rallied strongly even as rates have come down only modestly — underscoring that safe-haven demand, not just rate cuts, has driven much of gold’s recent strength. So far, safe-haven demand has been strong enough that, despite positive real yields (interest rates after adjusting for inflation), gold prices have kept rising. For GDX, a stable-to-falling interest rate environment paired with solid gold demand creates favorable conditions for miners to maintain high margins.

GDX vs Physical Gold ETFs

Gold miner stocks have handily beaten physical gold stocks in the recent gold bull run. Year-to-date 2025, GDX has surged ~71% (through early August), whereas the iShares Gold Trust (IAU), a proxy for physical gold, is up only about 29%. In the 1-year horizon as well, GDX outperformed IAU, returning ~63.9% vs ~39.6% for IAU. This 1.5-2x leverage for miners to gold prices is typical of the industry during downturns - an expert here gives an even higher figure. Like I noted before, that is mainly due to the miner's fixed cost structure. This underscores that GDX is a high-beta play on the gold price, suitable for investors who can stomach the ride.

GDX has a higher expense ratio than the large gold bullion ETFs. Net expense ratio is about 0.51% annually, compared to 0.40% for SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) and 0.25% for IAU (one of the lowest-cost gold funds). While GDX’s fee may sound reasonable for an equity ETF, it is roughly double the cost of holding physical gold via IAU. On the other hand, GDX does provide income (via periodic distributions of dividends from the mining stocks it holds), whereas GLD/IAU hold physical gold that yields nothing. While the yield is modest (around 1-2% annualized), it is a small bonus that bullion ETFs do not offer. GDX is also large in size in both AUM ($17+ billion) and heavy trading volume. While that is dwarfed by GLD’s $100 billion AUM, it is still sizable and has a tight bid-ask spread.

As for GDX’ portfolio, it holds a basket of 60+ gold and precious metal mining stocks worldwide. This tracks the NYSE Arca Gold Miners Index. The fund is market cap weighted, which means larger companies carry greater weight. Newmont Corp (NEM), the world’s largest gold miner, is currently the top holding at 13.5% of the portfolio. Many other major names comprise the portfolio, and these miners are distributed across Canada, the U.S., Australia, South Africa, and more. This diversified mix gives GDX a broad participation in the industry’s ups and downs, from senior producers to smaller ones. A sister fund, GDXJ, holds more small-cap junior miners. GDX’s constituents are established companies with producing mines and relatively lower geopolitical risk (though some holdings do operate in emerging markets).

A quick look at GDX’ portfolio

Top GDX stocks include Newmont (13.5%), Agnico Eagle (12.0%), Wheaton Precious Metals (7.7%), plus Barrick, Franco-Nevada, and Gold Fields among its 68 holdings. Dividend yield across these stocks range from ~0.9% for Franco-Nevada to nearly 1.9% for Barrick. Newmont (1.45%), Agnico Eagle (~1.3%), and Wheaton (~1.6%) add steady income alongside capital appreciation potential.

Several miners are actively buying back stock. In 2025, Newmont authorized a $3 billion program, Barrick launched a $1 billion buyback, Kinross pledged $500 million, and Agnico Eagle repurchased $150 million in H1.

In terms of valuation metrics, on a trailing PE basis, Newmont (~12–14x) and Barrick (~14–17x) trade at notable discounts to the S&P 500’s ~29–30x. Agnico Eagle carries a higher 23x multiple because of its high-growth potential. Wheaten trades at 50x PE, but it is not a traditional miner, rather it is a streaming and royalty company providing finance to miners. The S&P 500 forward P/E is ~22x, making some miners look attractively priced relative to the broader market.

Overall, GDX offers exposure to well-capitalized companies with healthy balance sheets, ongoing buybacks, and dividend income — all while trading at reasonable valuations compared to the index.

Why choose Gold Miners over Physical Gold?

Owning physical gold has its own attraction, but there are several compelling reasons to consider a stock like GDX instead.

First, as I already noted, gold mining companies have operational leverage. This effectively means mining stocks are able to magnify gold’s moves, demonstrating a beta of about 2-3 times gold prices. We noted how this was seen in the recent bull market. So, if you are bullish on gold, it may be a better approach to own gold miners instead of physical gold. GDX allows one to tap that leverage in a diversified manner, rather than picking individual mining stocks.

The second item is that physical gold does not pay any income or dividends. GDX does, and the especially high profit margins have enabled mining companies to give stock returns through debt repayments, share buybacks, and dividend income. In recent months, several top miners have rewarded shareholders: Barrick announced a $1 billion stock buyback after doubling its free cash flow in Q4, AngloGold Ashanti declared a dividend five times higher than the prior year, and Gold Fields signaled it may buy back shares as well. This isn’t something you could expect with physical gold.

There is room for multiple expansions in these gold miner stocks because, despite their strong fundamentals, investor skepticism has kept them undervalued. Many trade at lower valuation multiples than the broader market. For example, as of mid-2025, many senior producers have price-to-earnings (P/E) ratios in the low teens (or even single digits) and enterprise value/EBITDA around ~6–7×, which is roughly half the S&P 500’s multiple. Like a recent article said, “Gold prices have surged to over $3,300/oz; however, many equities in the sector still trade as if gold were under $2,000.” For investors, GDX thus offers not only a play on gold prices but also a play on mean-reversion in mining equity valuations.

GDX helps spread your risks across dozens of mines and projects around the world. When gold prices rise, previously marginal projects become economical. Unlike that, a physical gold ETF has no growth. One ounce of gold in an etf today will remain an ounce after a year. But miners can - and often do - discover two ounces of gold for every one they mine. This is called the reserve replacement rate, and several top miners have over 100% such rates. Such discoveries add value beyond current gold prices. This upside optionality you can only get in an active miner stock like GDX, not in gold.

Miners can be a leveraged inflation hedge, distributing cash and faring better under moderate inflation, using the enlarged spread between gold prices and fixed costs. Moreover, some of GDX portfolio companies pay dividends that tend to go up as gold rises. This means investors can get a yield during the holding period – GDX distributed roughly 2% of NAV in dividends over the past year – which is an advantage over holding gold bars that yield zero.

Risks and Considerations

There are potential downsides to owning GDX. Let me discuss some of these below.

Firstly, as I have noted before, historically, GDX has moved 2–3x the percentage change in gold, both up and down. These miner stocks usually overshoot to the downside when gold prices fall. As the following source notes, “The November 2024 election cycle clearly demonstrated this tendency, as political uncertainty triggered an 8% gold decline that produced a disproportionate 23.4% collapse in mining equities—a sharp 2.9x downside leverage.”

Although I did say that mining costs are more or less fixed, they do rise or fall somewhat, driven by inflation. Energy, equipment, and labor become expensive, which may erode profit margins. Oil prices are a major driver of mining costs, and in a downturn economy, oil prices may increase. GDX is well-diversified to weather any single company’s problems, but if there’s an industrywide cost hike, margins may get squeezed. Mining companies take time to respond to fresh demand. New operations may take years. Thus, they may not always be able to capitalize on today’s high gold prices.

Global unrest does drive gold prices higher. But there could be situations where the very nature of the unrest hurts specific miners. Some of these GDX portfolio miners work in sensitive places. In such places, government changes, resource nationalism, or even conflict can happen, causing demands for higher taxes, permit delays, or even asset seizures.

A recent example is the tax disputes in Mali which hurt the operations of Randgold, a subsidiary of Barrick Gold. The government of Mali enacted a new law in 2023, providing the country with a larger share of gold profits. However, in the status quo scenario, Barrick owned 80% of the mines while Mali owned 20%. Barrick claimed to have spent $16 billion in the mine. They took various government entities to an International Arbitration Court after their officials were apparently detained by the government on “unfounded charges.” Bottomline, local politics can hurt globally diversified miners.

Lastly, let's not forget the higher expense ratio for GDX, which is almost double that of IAU, although, at 0.51%, still quite low. That higher price is what you pay for the diversification and higher return potential.

Conclusion

I hope the above discussion has shown us why the VanEck Gold Miners ETF offers a solid opportunity in this market. Softening US dollars, easing of rates, and geopolitical conflicts all come together to make GDX a solid bet for investors looking to hedge. Of course, this is so as long as they remember the principal risk I have discussed - GDX could be more volatile than physical gold. So, use it as a portfolio diversifier, and only as long as you are confident in gold’s strength. GDX can complement, but not replace, bullion holdings, because, while it has higher potential, it also has higher risks.

data.title: Brazil's Realignment: From Economic Stagnation To Strategic Relevance
data.description:
By Mark Barnes, PhD, Head of Global Investment Research, Americas, FTSE Russell | Alex Nae, M.Sc, Quantitative Research Analyst, Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell

Brazil has quietly shifted from years of stagnation to a position of renewed economic relevance, with policy reform and disciplined monetary management driving stability. Resilient domestic demand and a balanced commodity base have supported growth that now outpaces many major economies. Low valuations, shifting sector dynamics and moderate global correlations position Brazil as a distinctive component in global equity allocations.

    Global trade is undergoing profound changes shaped by geopolitical realignments, persistent inflation pressures, tariff-based policies, and divergent monetary policies. This evolving landscape underscores both fragmentation and heightened interdependence, as reduced coordination and greater economic isolation limit the effectiveness of collective responses to disruptions, amplifying the global impact of economic shocks. Against this backdrop, economies demonstrating internal resilience and structural balance are increasingly relevant. Latin America’s largest economy, Brazil, emerges as a notable example, transitioning from nearly a decade of stagnation towards macroeconomic stabilisation and internal reform. This insight summarizes a recent paper we published on Brazil’s economic realignment and its implications within the current global context.

    Structural roots of Brazil’s economic stagnation (2011–2020)

    Brazil’s economic stagnation between 2011 and 2020 was characterised by policy missteps, declining productivity, and persistent inflation, culminating in stagflation, a damaging combination of economic stagnation and inflationary pressures. Driven by premature austerity measures, deregulated fuel pricing, significant currency volatility, and extensive political instability, this period severely undermined economic confidence and investment activity.

    Exhibit 1: Brazil’s stagflation period: inflation and GDP growth

    Inflation during this period was predominantly driven by cost-push factors such as regulated price increases, wage indexation, and food price volatility. To contain inflation, Brazil’s central bank sharply increased the benchmark SELIC rate from 10% to 14.25% by 2015. While successful in moderating inflation expectations, this tightening significantly constrained domestic credit and further deepened the economic slowdown.

    Exhibit 2: Brazil SELIC benchmark rate and inflation

    Political developments exacerbated these challenges. The extensive corruption revelations from Operação Lava Jato increased Brazil’s political risk premium. A subsequent leadership change in 2016 led to market stabilisation and initial structural reforms, yet the economic recovery remained modest and vulnerable.

    Commodity dependence and economic vulnerability

    Part of Brazil’s economic structure historically relied on commodity exports, primarily oil, iron ore, and soybeans. This reliance exposed the economy to volatility and risks associated with global commodity price fluctuations, currency appreciation, and sectoral imbalance.

    Exhibit 3: Nominal Brazilian GDP (billions USD) and GDP contribution of oil, iron, and soybeans

    In 2024, these commodities represented 5.1% of GDP and 14.3% of exports. However, diversification across multiple commodities helps moderate shocks, with, for example, weaker oil prices being offset by stronger soybean exports to China. Nonetheless, these sectors remain tied to global demand, and a broad slowdown can weigh on commodity demand. Services, industrial activity, and domestic consumption provide additional stability, helping sustain domestic demand and employment even during periods of external weakness.

    Macroeconomic stabilisation and policy reform momentum

    Since 2023, Brazil has implemented substantial policy reforms aimed at fiscal consolidation, tax efficiency, and economic stability. The introduction of a new fiscal framework limiting spending growth to 70% of revenue increases and a simplified VAT system improved Brazil’s fiscal credibility. Exhibit 4 shows the recent improvement in credit and risk rating.

    Exhibit 4: Brazil Oxford Economics’ credit rating and Datastream’s risk rating

    Monetary policy complemented these reforms with consistent anti-inflationary measures. Brazil’s central bank maintained elevated interest rates, significantly higher than regional peers, contributing to a marked reduction in inflation, from a peak of 12% in early 2022 to approximately 5% by June 2025. Nonetheless, Brazil faces ongoing fiscal challenges, including elevated public debt (76.5% of GDP) and rising domestic credit levels, necessitating continued policy monitoring.

    Exhibit 5: CPI inflation rates, Latin America

    Growth driven by domestic demand amid external pressures

    Brazil’s GDP growth accelerated from 2.6% in 2023 to 3.2% in 2024, outpacing most major economies in the chart and demonstrating consistent momentum despite a challenging global backdrop. Key domestic indicators such as retail sales growth (+8%), declining unemployment rates (from 11% in 2022 to 6% in 2025), and stable industrial production (+2.8%) underpin this economic resilience, reflecting strong internal consumption dynamics.

    Exhibit 6: Recent regional GDP growth, 2023 and 2024

    Equity market dynamics: the FTSE Brazil Index

    The FTSE Brazil Index provides insight into Brazil’s economic transition. After initially outperforming global indices due to a commodity rally, the index subsequently declined by 13% from its 2023 peak due to tightening global financial conditions and falling commodity prices. However, recent performance indicators, such as a notable 27% return in 2025 through June, which was 17% higher than the FTSE All-World and 16% higher than the Emerging index, highlight improving domestic economic fundamentals and market confidence.

    Exhibit 7: Cumulative returns, rebased USD

    Industry performance within the index reveals Brazil’s economic realignment. Domestically focused industries such as Financials (8.1%), Utilities (3.1%), and Industrials (2%) positively contributed to performance, while cyclical sectors tied to global conditions, such as Energy (-1.3%) and Basic Materials (-2.2%), detracted from returns. This performance aligns with Brazil’s pivot to industries driven by domestic economic activity rather relying overly on global cyclical factors. Lower industry performance in Oil and Basic Materials is in line with global trends.

    Exhibit 8: 12-Month industry-weighted contributions to FTSE Brazil return, 2024 to 2025

    Valuation and earnings outlook

    Brazilian equities appear to be undervalued relative to global peers. As of 2025, Brazil’s forward P/E ratio stands at 7.9, significantly below both the All-World average of 18.5 and the Emerging Market average of 13.2. Despite macroeconomic progress and improved earnings performance, this valuation gap has persisted over time. Since 2022, Brazil’s forward P/E has risen from 6.1 to 7.9, yet the discount to broader benchmarks remains wide, pointing to a potential re-rating if current momentum holds.

    Exhibit 9: 12-Month Forward price to earnings ratios by index

    Strategic trade positioning and revenue diversification

    The paper’s analysis of revenue composition reveals Brazil’s increasing domestic orientation, with approximately 72.5% of FTSE Brazil Index revenue derived from domestic operations. Over the past year, revenue exposure to China decreased significantly (-21.5%), while revenue from the United States increased (+17.4%), underscoring effective diversification efforts amid changing global trade dynamics. Initially, Brazil’s balanced trade surplus with China and modest US deficit suggested limited tariff risk. However, the US has since signalled a potential 50% tariff on Brazilian goods independent of the US’s surplus with Brazil. While this raises near-term uncertainty, recent US negotiations with other countries suggest that outcomes are not set in stone.

    Exhibit 10: Brazil trade balance with China and the US (millions of USD)

    Portfolio diversification and correlation characteristics

    Finally, from a portfolio perspective, Brazil’s equities offer some diversification due to moderate correlation with major global equity markets. Brazil’s equity market demonstrates a lower correlation with US equities (0.58) and Emerging Markets (0.75), attributed to its sector composition emphasizing cyclical, domestic, and commodity-oriented industries. This diversification supports stronger return potential and enhances the balance of global equity allocations.

    Exhibit 11: Correlation of asset class returns, monthly USD, 5-years through June 2025

    Correlation Matrix FTSE Brazil US Dev ex-US All-World Emerging
    FTSE Brazil 1 0.58 0.68 0.67 0.75
    US 0.58 1 0.86 0.95 0.72
    Dev ex-US 0.68 0.86 1 0.97 0.85
    All-World 0.67 0.95 0.97 1 0.85
    Emerging 0.75 0.72 0.85 0.85 1

    Source: FTSE Russell and LSEG, as of June 30, 2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future returns. Please see the end for important legal disclosures.

    Conclusion: Brazil’s economic realignment

    Brazil’s economic realignment marks a shift from prolonged stagnation toward greater macroeconomic stability and structural improvement. Recent reforms and disciplined monetary policy have strengthened the outlook, but persistent inflation pressures, high public debt, and external uncertainties require sustained policy discipline.

    A diversified commodity base, resilient domestic demand, and credible fiscal and monetary frameworks enhance Brazil’s capacity to navigate global shocks, though exposure to global growth cycles remains significant. Within a more fragmented but interconnected global economy, Brazil offers an instructive example of resilience and structural adjustment, while facing ongoing challenges that will test the durability of these gains.
