data.title: I Am Buying The Big Dip With 11% Yield: Greystone Housing
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

One of the biggest mistakes that new investors or seasoned investors who've gotten a little lazy with their analysis make is assuming that all companies operate similarly. They also believe that all companies should be readily compared to the general market indexes, which are now overwhelmingly and over-weighted in favor of big tech, aside from the Dow Jones Industrial Index (DJI), which is weighted based on share price, not market capitalization. The other two major indexes, the Nasdaq Composite Index (COMP:IND) and the S&P 500 (SP500), are overwhelmingly weighted in massive tech companies. Meaning that, while these companies could be performing well and the rest of the economy performing terribly, they will still climb. Inversely, when these companies perform poorly and fall, the indexes may decline even as the rest of the market is doing well.

This is one reason why market indexes have become a less effective means of measuring overall market performance, especially when you're no longer invested in the major indexes. Many immediate income investors, like myself, are invested in companies that are not in or related to these market indexes. So, while the index may move up or down in a given day, our portfolio may have very little correlation or movement that's similar to it.

Today, I want to take a look at something that's not in any of those market indexes. It is a common equity that primarily holds a basket of fixed-income assets, meaning it will be more impacted by credit defaults and interest rate changes. Usually, when interest rates fall, long-dated maturity bonds climb in value, and likewise, when interest rates climb, those bonds will fall in trading value. This won't change how they mature at PAR, but it can change their value in the interim. As you can imagine, if interest rates climbed rapidly like we've seen recently, that could cause a portfolio of these types of holdings to fall in trading value, but not necessarily in their income generation.

Let's dive in!

House Your Income

Greystone Housing Impact Investors (NYSE:GHI), yielding 11.5%, focuses on holding a portfolio of mortgage revenue bonds. To supplement this income stream, GHI also has a portfolio of properties in development that it oversees, including construction and leasing, before selling them.

Note: This article is an update to our coverage of GHI and should be seen as a continuation of the coverage series.

GHI reported a net income loss of $0.35/unit and CAD (Cash Available for Distribution) of $0.25/unit. The main difference between the two was a net provision for credit losses of slightly over $9 million. GHI recently started using the CECL (Current Expected Credit Loss) standard, which requires a provision of credit losses when a loan becomes distressed. The provision for credit loss flows through the income statement, even though it is not a cash expense. Even if a loss is ultimately realized, it will result as a loss in book value – it isn't a cash expense that GHI has to pay.

The provision for credit loss covers a $624,000 loan to a non-profit Opportunity South Carolina and $8.7 million in three MRBs that Opportunity South Carolina owes for the renovation of multi-family apartments.

The three apartment properties that are securing the MRBs are The Park at Sondrio, The Park at Vietti, and Windsor Shores Apartments: Source

In the earnings call, management discussed that the cash flow from the properties was underperforming underwritten expectations and that they were working with the borrower to reach a resolution. In total, the amount owed on the three properties is slightly over $78 million.

We will have to see if that situation gets resolved. These types of issues pop up occasionally, and previously, they didn't have any impact on GHI's income statements until/unless a loss was actually realized. With the CECL standard, losses are recognized when a loan has issues and then reversed if the borrower can right the ship.

GHI has nearly $1.5 billion in assets, so if the $9 million loss materializes, it is hardly the end of the world. However, when $9 million is run through the income statement and GHI has $23.5 million/quarter in gross revenue, it looks like a lot.

In terms of cash flow, GHI has two primary strategies. Their MRB (mortgage-revenue bond) strategy involves investing in MRBs and collecting interest. While these investments carry some credit risk, the default rates tend to be relatively low and the recovery rates are relatively high. During the Great Financial Crisis, GHI did foreclose on some properties and operated them for a few years before selling them.

The primary factor that has been impacting GHI in recent years is higher interest rates. GHI profits from the difference between what they borrow at and the interest they collect. GHI has hedged a lot of their interest rate risk, but higher interest rates are a negative and lower interest rates will be a positive:

If we compare this to GHI's interest rate sensitivity in Q2 2021, before they hedged, we can see that it was far more sensitive: Source

A 200 bps increase in rates was expected to reduce Net Interest Income by $5.185 million. Interest rates have gone up more than 200 bps since then, and that has contributed to the decline in GHI's earnings from this segment, although to a much smaller extent than projected because GHI decided to enter into some interest-rate swaps. Declining interest rates would be a slight positive for GHI's earnings immediately, and it would become a greater positive as GHI allowed its interest rate swaps to roll off.

Right now, the market is projecting a 90% probability that the Fed starts cutting in September, but the rate-cut carrot has been dangling in the near future for a year now.

GHI's interest rate swaps mature slowly over the next several years. Note that the swaps maturing from 2025 to 2029 are in the 3.25%-3.5% ballpark.

So, the swaps are roughly breaking even when the Fed cuts to 3.25%-3.5% which would be four rate cuts. If the Fed were to cut dramatically to say 0%, then GHI might pay the fee to exit the contracts. However, if the Fed reduces rates slowly, then GHI will probably allow most of these contracts to mature naturally and pay higher interest during the period that SOFR rates are below 3.25%.

As a result, if the Fed cuts rates now, it is a slight positive for GHI on the unhedged portion of its debt. And once the Fed rate is below 3.25%, then the benefit will increase over time as these swaps mature.

This dynamic will likely continue to be the primary determinant of GHI's earnings from this segment.

The other segment that GHI has been increasing its allocation to is the development side. Unlike the MRB business, which is focused on apartments with affordable housing components, the development side of the business is for-profit multi-family apartment complexes. GHI provides capital to a partner, who constructs the apartments, leases them up, and then looks to sell them.

The gains in this segment primarily come from when the property is sold. Construction has been completed on six of these properties, with another three under construction and two more in the planning stages:

This business was responsible for very large gains in 2021-2023 and are what drove the rising dividend and supplemental dividends that GHI paid. However, over the past two years, sales activity in commercial real estate has slowed down. This has shown itself in the length of time before properties sell, and also in lower prices for the properties that have sold. Note that the average price for the properties sold this year has been $159k/unit, while in 2023, properties were selling for an average of $183k/unit.

While we've seen some green shoots in commercial real estate, it is still slower than average and certainly much slower than it was post-COVID. Declining interest rates are typically a positive for real estate activity, although an economic slowdown can be a counterweight to that. Consulting the Magic 8-ball, "The future is uncertain". GHI relies on occasional property sales from this segment of its business, and that uncertainty is why it reduced its distribution to $0.30/quarter. This implies that they expect to continue seeing the occasional sale, but they won't be frequent and might not be for premium prices.

While some investors might be concerned about this, we've been on this ride with GHI before. During the COVID-19 pandemic, all property sales came to a screeching halt, and GHI reduced its distribution to $0.17/quarter, and its share price bottomed about where it is trading right now. This is weird to us because it's paying a much higher dividend, and the outlook might not be the most clear, but it certainly isn't the mayhem and fear that we saw during COVID! When GHI started selling properties again, they raised the distribution right back up and ended up paying out more in distributions from 2020 to 2023 than they would have paid if the distribution had been flat the whole period. The reason is that they were patient and waited for market conditions to be right to get the best gains. They were willing to reduce the distribution and retain capital in 2020, and that drove them to have higher realized gains in 2021-2023. And management was happy to send those gains along to shareholders as distributions.

Right now is another low ebb, and it doesn't appear to be nearly as low of an ebb as we saw in 2020 for the distribution. Rate cuts, if they come, will help out a little bit. A recovery in the multi-family sector would help out a lot, but when that might happen is harder to predict.

I remember talking about GHI (then ATAX) in 2021 and 2022, and the comment section would be riddled with commentary along the lines of "It's too late, why weren't you recommending this in 2020?". My response was, "I did, maybe you missed it." Well, here we are again. GHI is trading at crazy low fire-sale prices. It is within spitting distance of where it was trading at its lowest in 2020, even though it is generating higher earnings and paying a much higher distribution than it was then. Life doesn't always give second chances, but it is giving a second chance to buy GHI at these bargain prices.

So, when I'm writing about GHI in 2026 or 2027, whenever the cycle turns and investors are back to valuing cash flow at high prices, I'll point you back to this article. I did talk about it.

Conclusion

GHI shouldn't be viewed as simply a standard common equity investment, because the vast majority of its returns over time are likely to come from its tax-favored distributions. This is due to its portfolio of mortgage revenue bonds, which is also supplemented by its holdings that are for sale. Overwhelmingly, GHI trades and moves more like a fixed equity or fixed income investment or a bond portfolio than it does alongside the major market indexes:

GHI provides outsized returns when compared to market-wide bond ETFs (BND) or Treasury Notes (TLT). These types of investments would provide a more accurate comparison to the types of returns you can expect to receive from GHI over the long run.

When it comes to your retirement portfolio, the question doesn't become, "How do I maximize every single dollar in my portfolio to get the highest mathematical return?" It may be for some, and that's great! However, for all of us who are human and have blood running through our veins, there will be a psychological component that is extremely impactful. For many, they would rather collect dividends and distributions without having to sell a single share, rather than live in the fear of the next market correction or bear market drop, watching their nest egg rapidly diminish in unrealized value because they were holding on to that unrealized gain as a potential win in the long run. For myself and countless others who are members of the High Dividend Opportunities community, we prefer to accept our returns in cold, hard cash and enjoy them as such.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Meta Is A Strong Business At A Fair Price Even Without The Upside From AI
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc (NASDAQ:META) has a strong business moat that is least likely to be negatively affected by AI but has the potential to benefit from it vastly if its AI strategy can be successfully deployed. On the creator side, AI can help brands generate ads more efficiently, optimize ads campaigns, and increase target accuracy. On the consumer side, the low-hanging fruit is to enter the search business by attracting users to conduct searches on its Family of Apps powered by Meta AI with the potential to transform it into a personal assistant in the future.

Simple business to understand that can last a long time

Meta’s major business is straightforward to understand — it makes money by selling advertising space on its Family of Apps (FoA). In many ways, this is like the traditional newspaper business: the primary product is not the content itself, but the audience’s attention. When people want to know what is going on around them or are just bored, they turn to Meta's apps.

The goal of the business is simple: grow the user base and capture their time share. This will initiate a flywheel dynamic: The more attention a platform generates (user base * time span), the more advertisers will use the platform to display ads, the more ads Meta will have in its inventory, and the more relevant the ads become to its users.

Unlike Google, whose core ad business is tied to search — a functional utility — Meta’s advertising is built on emotional utility. Functional utility, as of now, has the risk of being replaced by a new channel of obtaining the needed information - AI. With AI, people may no longer need to use Google to search for results, but instead they would just ask their AI Chatbot. Chatbots are more efficient than humans in going through vast amount of data and summarize the most relevant information to present to the users. Based on an article by Barron's, Google Search is fading at an alarming rate due to the emergence of AI chatbots redefining how users search for information. I will discuss how Meta can make a play during this AI transformation later in the AI section.

AI can replace or disrupt functional utilities like search by offering faster and more concise answers with information gathered across sources. However, it is less likely to replace human connection, entertainment, and ways to just kill time - exactly what Meta's apps offer. For this reason, I believe Meta’s core advertising model — built on emotional engagement at massive scale — is likely to remain resilient even if AI becomes ubiquitous.

Durable competitive moat - Seven Powers analysis

The concept of the Seven Powers comes from the book 7 Powers: The Foundations of Business Strategy by Hamilton Helmer, first published in 2016. The book highlights 7 specific, enduring sources of business advantage that can help a business create and sustain exceptional returns over the long term - in other words, provide a strong business moat. The seven powers are:

Scale economies

Per unit costs decline as volume increases

Network economies

Value realized by a customer increases as the userbase increases

Counter positioning (invalid for established players)

New superior business model that incumbents cannot mimic due to the anticipated cannibalization of their existing business

Switching costs

Customers expect a greater loss than the value they gain from switching to an alternative

Branding

higher perceived value to an objectively identical offering due to historical information about them

Cornered resources

Has preferential access to a coveted resource that independently enhances value

Process power

Organization and activity set enables lower costs and/or superior products that can only be matched by an extended commitment

Source: 7 Powers: The Foundations of Business Strategy by Hamilton Helmer

Out of the 7 powers, I believe Meta possesses 3 that are very important contributing to its moat - network economies, switching costs, and process power

Network economies are simple to understand but very powerful - the larger the userbase, the more useful the platform becomes. As of Q1, 2025, Meta's all products combined DAU is about 3.43 billion. To put that into perspective, as of early 2025, the world's total internet users is about 5.56 billion - about 60% of people use one of Meta's apps every day (and this is when Meta is banned in China). If we take China's 1.1 billion internet users out of the base, Meta's penetration increases to an astonishing 77%. With this much user base, Meta can 1) increase its bargaining power towards brands, 2) accumulate a diversified ads inventory that will display just the right ads to different users, 3) optimize and improve its ads algorithm continuously and systematically backed by loads of data, and 4) re-direct users to other future apps in case new competitors emerge.

Switching costs for users are also very high— people want to be on the same platform as everyone they care about, whether that’s family, friends, celebrities, or influencers, and they are less likely to switch to other platforms unless everyone else does. The real risk comes if everyone were to jump ship at once, but this is unlikely unless an alternative platform offers massive incentives to attract a critical mass of influencers simultaneously — similar to what TikTok did. Even then, such a platform would compete primarily for the entertainment portion of the experience, not the connectivity component. In other words, TikTok cannot replace Facebook because it is not a social platform, but purely a media platform. It also means that Meta, with Reels or other future products, can win back some time share by investing heavily in influencers themselves, and Meta has lots of capital to do that.

Process power is not always visible to end users, but it is a critical pillar of Meta’s competitive moat - In Meta’s case, it lies in the company’s ability to consistently deliver just the right ad, in the right amount, to the right user, at the right price for the advertiser - the value is in the system. The system consists of highly refined and optimized machine learning models that went through iterations after iterations, real-time bidding algorithms, and continuous experimentation. Over more than a decade, Meta has built a closed feedback loop between user engagement data, ad performance outcomes, and advertiser bidding behavior. Every impression shown feeds back into this system, enabling constant fine-tuning of targeting accuracy, frequency capping, and price optimization. Replicating this process is not just a matter of hiring data scientists or buying servers — it requires years of iterative learning, organizational know-how, and tight integration between product, engineering, and sales teams. In other words, it takes time and time is not something competitors can just come in and copy. On top of this, Meta is not shy of paying for top talents (currently doing in the current AI race). Meta has the huge capital required to defend its R&D team and poach new talents when fighting a competitor.

In conclusion, I can say with high certainty that Meta's business moat will continue to exist for decades, and instead of deteriorating, it will become even stronger as time passes by.

Potential room for further growth

Meta's revenue driver comes from the following: 1) increase in user base, 2) increase in average time spent on Meta's platform (impacting ad impressions) and 3) increase in revenue per ad impression

User base will continue to increase organically due to a) increase in total internet users, and b) increasing penetration rate; however, the growth rate will likely to be low given Meta's existing large user base.

Average time spent on Meta's platform is a mixed story. According to Statista, the peak average minutes per day spent on social media peaked in 2023. This means that the overall social media tailwind is likely passed and it is time to fight for market share (i.e. time share).

According to BusinessDasher, Meta's Instagram is still lagging behind TikTok, which means there is room to grow. However, this is likely to be a long and difficult process (if the government does implement a ban) since TikTok has a head start and resources from China (where Meta is banned) to compete with Meta. I believe one key reason TikTok caught Meta off guard was its initial growth in China — a market where Meta had little presence and therefore paid limited attention — allowing TikTok to amass capital, refine its product, and expand globally. Even so, Meta responded quickly by launching Reels and leveraging Instagram’s massive user base to mitigate much of the competitive impact.

There's always the possibility that Meta will acquire other social media startups to boost its user base and time spent on its platform

Despite average time spent on Meta's platform plateauing, ad impressions is increasing at about 5 to 10% year over year

Period

Year-over-Year Ad-Impression Growth

Q1 2025

+5% from the prior year

Q2 2025

+11% from the prior year

Q4 2024 (Quarter)

+6% from the prior year

Full Year 2024

+11% from the prior year

Although promising, this trend is unlikely to continue unless the average time spent on Meta’s apps also increases. Ad load on most of Meta’s apps is already high within their respective formats, leaving limited room for further expansion. The main exceptions are Reels, Messenger, and WhatsApp. Reels is still competing head-to-head with TikTok, so maintaining a good user experience is critical (i.e., less ads). For WhatsApp, Meta is exploring ways to display more ads, but user reception remains uncertain.

Revenue per ad impression has been increasing over the past two quarters as well as for the full year 2024; This upward trend will almost certainly continue in the long term. With Google facing challenges from AdWords, brands may even shift some investment towards Meta, driving average price per ad growth even higher.

Period

Average Price Per Ad Growth

Q1 2025

+10%

Q2 2025

+9%

Full year 2024

10%

On the creative side, AI tools now help advertisers automatically generate multiple ad variations, adapt creatives to different placements, and run multivariate tests at scale. This not only improves campaign performance for advertisers but also increases the perceived value of each impression. As a result, Meta can justify higher average ad prices without materially increasing ad load, thus preserving the user experience. One key important aspect about these tools is that they also benefit from scale - i.e. the more users use them, the better they will become. The most prominent AI advertising tool is Meta Advantage+, and with generative AI, Meta provides brands with more efficient ways to generate campaigns and optimize their deployment.

Advantage+ has reduced the time Saxx spends identifying leads and setting up campaigns, said Kevin Meikle, director of digital marketing. That gives the underwear brand’s marketing team more time to test other marketing channels and work on creative strategy, Meikle said. - WSJ reports

Though there are certain aspects Meta still needs to improve on, including increasing the accuracy of its target audience, more flexibility in alternating how an ads feel, and control where the ads are displayed. These are problems to work on and can only be solved when you have a large user base and enough data.

Able management

Personally, I always liked companies with founders in charge, especially founders who have more than 3+ years of experience running and growing the company after it reaches large scale. Zuckerberg has demonstrated both capability to grow Meta's core business and flexibility to invest in emerging technology and compete with new entrants. He showed that Meta is quick and capable enough to adapt to new changes (although I prefer business that does not require changes to stay successful), as in the case of TikTok competition and Generative AI.

Another metric I look at is the difference in compensation package between the CEO and the top management - smaller gap tends to result in better management (although not always). Based on public figures, Zuckerberg's total compensation is approximately $27.2 million in total for 2024, and the top executives pay are shown below:

  • COO: Javier Olivan — ~$25.6 million (~94% of CEO pay)
  • CPO: Christopher Cox — ~$23.5 million (~86% of CEO pay)
  • CTO: Andrew Bosworth — ~$23.5 million (~86%)
  • CFO: Susan Li — ~$23.5 million (~86%)

AI: The low-hanging fruit is Search

In an Instagram video posted in July, Zuckerberg explains that Meta's goal is to develop “personal superintelligence for everyone,” accessed through devices like “glasses that can see what we see, hear what we hear, and interact with us throughout the day.”

This goal is extremely ambitious, and if realized, it will change Meta's power over its users significantly - just imagine how powerful a counselor is to a commander. Although I am almost certain that this goal is not likely to be achieved, there is something near that Meta can gain ground on - and that is Search.

Search, or advertisement that comes with Search, is an incredibly lucrative business, and Google has remained the dominant player until now. With AI chatbot or assistant, the way people are searching for information is diversifying, and the gate Google has been controlling over the past few decades is deteriorating in prominence.

OpenAI, Perplexity, Claude, and Copilot are just some of the new gates emerging on the search market. I believe people will no longer go to a specific app to perform queries, but instead search for information on the social/news app/platform they spend the most of time on. This is already happening in China. In China, people are diverging from conducting searches in Baidu and instead are using WeChat, Red Note, and Toutiao (a news app) to search for information powered by LLMs. According to a report from Sina Finance, Red Note's average daily queries has doubled to 600 million by Q4 2024 compared to 300 million in mid-2023, mostly driven by integrating LLM models into its search engine.

If Meta is able to ramp up its Llama capabilities and penetrate the Search market, the upside can be very high. According to the Meta Q1 2025 Earnings Report and other sources gathered by AffiliateBooster, Meta AI's MAU has doubled to nearly 1 Billion since late 2024 (see table below). What fueled this rapid growth are Seamless Integration and Vast User Base. Meta has the time share, all it needs right now is to launch a product that is reasonably on par with other LLMs.

Time Period Reported/Estimated MAU Source Context
Late 2024 ~500 Million Demand Sage, AI Magazine reports
January 2025 ~700 Million EasyEquities, THE Journal reports
End of Q1 2025 Nearly 1 Billion Meta Q1 2025 Earnings Report

As of now, Google is still the dominant search provider, but the fast growth of OpenAI WAU has demonstrated that AI is an exponential phenomenon (comparable to the internet) and because it is possible, it is not impossible to redefine a users' search behavior despite Google's heavy investment and deployment of its own AI-based tools. The entrance to search will be diversified for certain. Meta, with its large user base and already popular apps across all age groups have a real shot penetrating into this lucrative market.

Zuckerberg is investing heavily in AI and it would be a tough game to compete in. However, Meta does have one advantage over non-social players, and that is Data. With people posting on Facebook and Instagram every day (public contents) Meta has easier access to these data than others (which need to scrape them through a tedious process). Also, the pace of improvement in AI models seems to be slowing down, which means Meta's chance of catching up is increasing. The current uncertainty lies in the capability of Alexandr Wang. He is more of a product person and comes from a data-labeling background. I am not sure if he can lead Meta's overall AI model development, but Zuckerberg, being the founder and has total control over the company, can replace Wang if progress is slow.

Financial strength and valuation

How much Meta will spend on AI is uncertain. It is estimated that Meta's 2025 Capex will reach around $70 billion. One thing for sure is that the AI space will consolidate, and as it consolidates, capex will return to normal. I've seen no reason why Meta's revenue will not continue to increase and its EBIT margin will drop in the future. Therefore, here I've presented some key figures in 2024/25 and my assumptions for 2030.

2024

2030

Revenue

$164 billion

$306 billion

EBIT margin

41%

43%

Capex

$37 billion, $70 billion for 2025

$65 billion

Pre-interest, Pre-acquisition cash flow to the company

$27 billion

$75 billion


Using a discount rate of 10% and terminal growth rate of 5% (above inflation) plus PV of about $225 billion from 2025 to 2029, we get Meta's EV is about 1.8 trillion, which translates to $750 per share. Meta's current WACC is 8%~11% so I chose 10% for the discount rate. A terminal growth rate of 5% above inflation reflects my confidence in Meta's pricing power for the long term. Meta's 2019 to 2024 revenue CAGR was 18% in absolute term and ~14% above US' inflation during the same period. Therefore, 5% should be more than achievable.

However, this valuation did not bake in the potential upside if Meta were to successfully enter the search market. If Meta's search were to reach 10% of Google's current scale, that would another ~$200 billion to its EV (Google's current market cap is about $2.5 trillion). This is just a rough estimate, but the point is that Meta's current valuation is fair given that it does not generate any additional return from its heavy investment in building a personal AI assistant. Given its current valuation, any success from its AI investment is extra return for investors.

Plus, Meta is repurchasing its shares at about $30 billion per year (although it will likely pause or reduce this as it invests in AI for the next few years). Overall, I think the existing business is at fair valuation and is relatively happy buying into Meta as a business at this valuation.

Foreseeable risks that will be game changers

The real game changer would not be Meta losing in the AI race, but a new type of social media platform that replaces how we consume social media today - similar to how AI search is currently replacing Google. But like I've analyzed before, the chance of this happening is very small, as Meta is able to move fast to defend its dominant position.

Another cyclical risk is the reduction in advertising spending by brands. This is likely if the US enters a significant recession. But then, all companies will likely suffer.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Meta is a simple business with a strong and growing moat in the era of AI and trading at a fair valuation. Any success from its AI investment is considered extra for investors.

data.title: Don't Buy TQQQ After A Big Run Higher: Signs Of Market Top
data.description:
ProShares UltraPro QQQ ETF (NASDAQ:TQQQ) is a 3x leveraged product, where nice gains in the Big Tech universe have translated into something akin to a gold mine for bulls. For sure, buying TQQQ when everyone is bearish, like late 2022 and early April of this year, has proven an astute time to jump into this ETF.

Yet, other instances of extreme optimism in the technology sector have proved rotten times to purchase a TQQQ position. Today, momentum data on the sharp stock market spike since April is fading fast. In fact, looking at the difference between Invesco QQQ ETF (QQQ) and Direxion NASDAQ-100 Equal Weight ETF (QQQE) trading activity, an important equity top could be close at hand.

TQQQ owns all of the NASDAQ 100 names, plus about $4 billion cash at last report (June 30th, 2025). The way it creates the 3x (300%) leverage on daily rebalancing is through swap contracts with major money center banks and the largest brokerage companies on Wall Street (too-big-to-fail businesses as counterparties).

My bearish logic for TQQQ is any type of stock market top in August, leveraging by a rate of 3x regular future equity declines, should be sold and avoided at all costs right now.

Let me explain the current toppy U.S. market condition and how negative performance can be difficult to overcome for investors acquiring TQQQ near an important NASDAQ 100 peak.

#1 - Don't Buy TQQQ Near A Top

It's rather obvious you should avoid TQQQ if the market is about to slide lower. Your losses (pain) will be around 3x that of the plain vanilla (100%-long invested) QQQ ETF.

What you may not completely understand is the math gets quite difficult to recover your losses (become whole again), if you buy and hold near a market top that lasts for a lengthy period of time. My summary is stick with the 100% QQQ product after a monster run higher, like we have experienced over the last four months, if you want exposure to Big Tech players.

Incredible Leverage Near A Bottom

I want to start out by highlighting the positives of this 3x leverage investment product when times are good. It's the main draw for investors/speculators. When you buy TQQQ before a sizable run higher in Big Tech names, huge returns are possible. The reason is the daily rebalance feature. It allows TQQQ to "compound" money, like a pyramid trading strategy.

For example, had you purchased a stake at Trump's trade war-scare bottom on April 7th, 2025, your TQQQ gains would have approached more like 4x the profits from the traditional QQQ, if held into the middle of August (130% vs. 37% for total returns).

Again, had you purchased in early January 2023, after the brutal 2022 yearly decline in QQQ names, a return greater than 4x the 100%-long ETF was your reward holding into the middle of December last year (431.5% vs. 101.5%). Yes, TQQQ works great when you buy it at the correct juncture in time.

Major Difficulties Recovering From A Top

The problem for investors arises if you buy near a market top (either unfortunate timing or being overly greedy). The math recovery consequence of owning TQQQ during historic percentage losses is clearly hard to overcome, outlined on the charts below.

For example, had you purchased TQQQ in mid-December 2024, and held until today, absorbing the huge hit (market slide) between February-April, you would have been better off in the regular QQQ design. As of today, nine months later, your TQQQ total return would have been miniscule and still lagging the QQQ buy-and-hold decision (2.3% vs. 8.2%).

Even worse, assuming a significant bear market is about to begin, stretched-out, longer-term equity downturns demolish future return potential in TQQQ. Had you bought in late 2021 (well after the party began), following the COVID stock market frenzy supported by "unlimited QE" responses at the Federal Reserve, the 2022 bear move in NASDAQ 100 equities created a greater than -80% loss in TQQQ over 12 months (peak to trough). Ouch!

Had you purchased QQQ instead of TQQQ in December 2021, today you would have doubled the net total return over three years and eight months (53.3% for QQQ vs. 26% for TQQQ). Despite the record run in AI-mania names off the January 2023 launch point, TQQQ at 3x leverage still has not been able to beat QQQ, if you purchased both products at the last market peak going into a lengthy bear decline.

So, like any stock/ETF, buying at the right time is equally important as buying the right sector or trend. You can review the wicked swings in TQQQ on a daily basis over the past year as confirmation of this concept. 3x products are definitely not suggested for the risk averse or those weak in the knees. Volatility is part of this ETF's DNA.

#2 - Peak NASDAQ 100 May Be Here

My issue for TQQQ today is I believe some sort of selloff is due for the U.S. stock market. Often, leading sectors of the market get hit by the most selling (as investors grab gains). I wrote about the increasing odds of a late-summer dump of 7% to 10% a few weeks ago. If anything, I am more worried now that a sizable drop is coming into the end of the year for the major indexes and averages, possibly a 15% to 20% correction.

New evidence has emerged that NASDAQ 100 ETFs are ready to turn lower. The primary argument is based on collapsing breadth in Big Tech names over the past several weeks, especially in the middle of August. Fewer and fewer individual stocks are participating in new all-time highs for the indexes. You can see this problem in a long-term chart.

Below I have drawn a 5-year chart comparing QQQ (which is heavily cap-weighted toward the Magnificent 7 names) to the equally-weighted QQQE design. Upside performance has been skewed toward just 10-15 names out of the 100 participating in the Big Tech index, particularly since the middle of 2023.

The main trading signal for a meaningful top in August? A massive skew toward only a few names rising in price (if any making new highs) happens on a daily chart comparison, where the equally-weighted design turns over and begins to fall in price/value. It's a classic Wall Street warning of upside exhaustion. When this occurs at the same moment as new index highs are achieved in the overall capitalization-weighted design, investor caution is warranted.

Breadth participation has been turning lower rather dramatically, in an unusual way lately. History provides plenty of evidence this is a dangerous market topping phenomenon.

For example, sharply diverging activity from QQQE occurred at the November-December 2021 peak in QQQ stocks, after being relatively in sync the whole year. Below I have marked with green arrows, the days QQQ reached a new all-time high, but QQQE had already turned lower, failing to confirm the strength.

In a similar fashion, late July into the middle of August 2025 has witnessed the same bearish technical activity (after being relatively in sync since April's low). Actually, the current stretch is screaming even louder to get out of the way and sell your QQQ-related positions, especially for traders. The December and February NASDAQ 100 peaks did not witness this situation, while May 2024 experienced a "false" peak signal for a few trading sessions.

Honestly, almost no individual Big Tech names have reached all-time highs over the last 2-3 trading days. In terms of bellwether non-confirmations, the market desperately needs better breadth in the days ahead, or a sizable price drop could be the only "technical analysis" conclusion for market direction into September (the worst month of the year statistically for U.S. equity performance).

Final Thoughts

If you believe both of my presented conditions to be true (#1 - TQQQ should be avoided near a market top and #2 - a peak is close), TQQQ should be taken off your buy research list until investors turn overly bearish again on the sector. Such could happen quickly in a mini-crash development (February-April 2025 as our prototype) or a bear market the drags on for a year or two (2022 as an example).

Assuming you have a different view of the AI-fueled boom in Big Tech names continuing, I understand you may choose to ignore my opinion on the matter. Just remember, given a large QQQ drop is approaching, TQQQ will again cycle down into a worst-in-class leveraged performer in your portfolio. In addition, buying at the top becomes a material impediment for long-term outperformance.

The dividend yield under 1% provides little cushion, if another 50%+ price drop in TQQQ is on deck. The annual 0.84% management expense is higher than I would like, while the ETF has to pay premium costs/values to create its swap-derived leverage. Even a flat NASDAQ 100 over the next year could generate small losses for TQQQ.

Consequently, I rate ProShares UltraPro QQQ ETF a Sell until a serious decline in price takes place (as most likely for an outcome) or breadth in the NASDAQ 100 acts considerably stronger for investors.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Amazon: Get Out While You Still Can
data.description:
While Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) was able to avoid a worst-case scenario so far and its shares managed to rise by around 6% since we rated it as a sell in May, we still think that the company is uninvestable right now. The latest implementation of reciprocal and basic-level tariffs by the Trump administration risks undermining the growth of Amazon’s eCommerce business in the future. In addition, the company’s record capital expenditures this year on expanding its AI infrastructure could also yield only limited returns, especially if the power issues that AWS faces are not resolved soon. Because of that, we continue to believe that Amazon is overvalued, and its shares are still a sell for us.

Tariffs & Competition Are Still An Issue

A couple of weeks ago, Amazon reported its Q2 results, which were fairly successful. The revenues during the quarter increased by 13.3% Y/Y to $167.7 billion, in part thanks to the good performance of the company’s North America segment, which generated $100.1 billion in revenues, up 11% Y/Y. Such a performance was achieved thanks to the lack of any major tariffs during the quarter. After all, the reciprocal tariffs that were announced on April 2 were quickly paused on April 9, thanks to which the impact on Amazon’s performance was minimal. However, going forward, things might change for the worse.

Just a couple of weeks ago, the Trump administration imposed reciprocal tariffs ranging from 10% to 41% on lots of countries and is unlikely to cancel them anytime soon. If costs for Amazon sellers rise as a result of this, then they might raise prices on their products. This could then prompt the consumers to decrease their purchases so that they avoid absorbing those potentially higher prices. That’s why it’s not a surprise that Amazon itself, in its latest 10-Q filing, noted that its sales could fluctuate due to tariff policy changes.

In the same filing, the company also mentioned that the China-based sellers are a big part of its ecosystem of third-party sellers, and the implementation of trade restrictions or tariffs could affect Amazon’s operating results. While the deadline for the trade deal between China and the United States has recently been extended again, the U.S. is still imposing a 30% tariff on Chinese goods, and some base tariff rate will nevertheless remain if the deal is reached later. After all, even the goods from the United Kingdom, Japan, and the European Union are still tariffed mostly at a 10% to 15% rate, despite the fact that the United States signed trade deals with its allies and partners recently.

More importantly, the effect of tariffs on prices is already increasing. The latest data shows that the Core CPI already accelerated to 3.1% Y/Y in July, which was above expectations. At the same time, the economists at Goldman Sachs believe that the impact of tariffs on consumer prices is only beginning, and the burden from tariffs will be passed to consumers in the future, which could result in the further acceleration of inflation. That’s why there’s a risk Amazon’s performance in the future won’t be as successful as it was in Q2.

When it comes to Amazon’s cloud business, investors were disappointed about its latest performance. While the sales of the AWS segment increased by 17.5% Y/Y in Q2, the growth rate was smaller in comparison to the growth rates of Google’s and Microsoft’s cloud business sales, which were up 32% Y/Y and 26% Y/Y, respectively. The issue for Amazon is that it might take several quarters for the company to secure enough power for its data centers to meet the demand. In the meantime, its competitors might continue to grow at a more aggressive rate and might make it harder for Amazon to maintain its leadership in the cloud business. That’s why it’s not a surprise that Amazon’s shares depreciated right after the Q2 results were announced, despite the fact that they were successful.

The Intrinsic Value of Amazon

All of those issues and challenges that Amazon faces will likely have a negative impact on the company’s performance in the foreseeable future. With its stock already trading at higher multiples than the sector’s average, we decided to figure out what Amazon’s real value is and created a valuation model.

The model is developed at a time when Amazon is trading at $222.69 per share, while its cash and debt data have been taken from the recent earnings report. The perpetual growth rate in the model is 3%, which is close to the historical GDP and inflation rate.

The discount rate is 8.36%, which was figured out after calculating Amazon’s cost of debt and cost of equity. Most of the numbers that were used to calculate the cost of debt are from the trailing twelve months. When calculating the cost of equity, we used a risk-free rate of 4.30%, a beta of 1.31, and the market return rate of 7.77%.

In the forecast table below, we increased the sales growth rate and EBIT margin by 1% in comparison to our last model from May. This is because the company had a decent performance in Q2 and might perform slightly better than we previously expected. However, considering that some sort of base-level tariff on imports to the United States is here to stay regardless of any trade deal, we expect a gradual decrease in the growth rate and EBIT margin over time. The tax rate, D&A, and the change in net working capital are mostly unchanged in comparison to the last model. The CapEx in 2025 mostly aligns with Amazon’s initial target of reaching around $100 billion in spending, after which we assume a slowdown in expenses.

This model shows that Amazon’s enterprise value is $1.37 trillion, while its equity value is $1.3 trillion. That translates to an intrinsic value of $120.68 per share. This is also higher in comparison to our last model from May, which then showed Amazon’s intrinsic value to be $104.90 per share. The increase right now is mostly due to a slight improvement in sales and EBIT expectations. However, even under new assumptions, Amazon remains overvalued and is uninvestable in our opinion.

Risks Could Be Mitigated

Despite the higher inflation rate, consumer confidence in the United States, along with retail sales in July, has also increased. This could indicate that the impact of higher tariffs might be manageable. If that’s the case, then it would make sense why Amazon outlined a fairly bullish Q3 sales target in the range between $174 billion and $179.5 billion. At this point, only time will tell how big an impact tariffs will have on the company’s real performance in the second half of the year.

As for the cloud business, if Amazon manages to quickly deal with the power issues, then it has a chance to continue to dominate the cloud business, where it had a 29% market share earlier this year. Considering that the cloud industry is also forecasted to grow at a double-digit rate in the following years, it makes sense to assume that Amazon still has plenty of opportunities for growth despite all the risks.

Final Thoughts

The depreciation of Amazon’s stock following the release of the Q2 report indicates that the market so far has been disappointed about the company’s growth opportunities. With tariffs now being implemented while AWS faces its own issues, it makes sense to remain bearish about Amazon’s stock, especially at the current price when it’s already overvalued. Because of that, we continue to rate Amazon as a sell.

data.title: flyExclusive: Cost Management Is Taking Shape, Not A Buy Yet
data.description:
flyExclusive (NYSE:FLYX), a company providing private travel solutions, is one of the few stocks on which I have a sell rating. Since my last report published in August 2024, the stock has lost 19% compared to the S&P 500’s 14.8% gain. Moreover, since I initiated coverage of the stock has lost over 44% of its value. The stock rose sharply following its Q2 2025 earnings report. In this article, I assess whether the stock offers a more compelling entry point at current price levels.

flyExclusive Revenues Increase, Losses Taper

Second quarter revenues increased 15.6% to $91.3 million but missed analyst expectations of $92 million in sales. The stock price, however, increased significantly, and I can understand why.

The previous times I analyzed flyExclusive, the company had some non-performing jets, and it planned on replacing those aircraft to drive financial performance. Over the past 12 months, the company has removed 24 out of the 37 non-performing jets. That means that loss-making jets are replaced by aircraft that do generate positive margins. The second quarter earnings show the financial progress made with top-line growth exceeding cost of revenues. Total operating expenses increased by a bit less than $3 million, but combined with the top line growth, the operating loss tapered from $21.8 million to $12.4 million.

flyExclusive derived revenues from four business lines. The first one is Jet Club and Charter, which essentially allows members to book flights at a fixed rate formula paid on top of their membership. That line of business saw revenue grow 9.6% to $79.5 million. The second line of business is fractional ownership, which saw nearly a doubling in revenues to $8.5 million. Maintenance activities grew 28% to $2.9 million, and aircraft management services is the fourth and newest line of business. Essentially, what we saw in the second quarter was an improvement in utilization, active member growth, and higher members per aircraft.

Operating cash burn improved from $42.2 million to $10.1 million, while capital expenditures decreased from $28.1 million to $4.1 million. This led to a free cash flow of negative $14.2 million compared to $70.3 million a year ago. The company had proceeds of $31.5 million compared to $23.7 million a year ago, leading to a free cash flow, including proceeds from sales of property and equipment, of positive $17.3 million compared to a burn of $46.6 million a year ago.

flyExclusive Stock Price Valuation

flyExclusive is not profitable on the EBITDA level. For that reason, using the EV/EBITDA valuation method, which is my preferred method to value stocks, is not suitable. Instead, I am using an EV/Sales valuation method. A complicating factor is that there are not that many peers for flyExclusive to establish typical EV/sales multiple. I am using a 1-2x multiple for established operators, 1.5x-3x for fast-growing operators with a membership component, and >2x for asset light operators. flyExclusive does not match either of these three categories exactly, but I do believe that a 2x EV/sales valuation would be fair.

Based on roughly 10% annual sales growth and a total of 80.1 million class A and class B shares, I would get to a stock price of $3.57 for this year, indicating a fair valuation and 10% and 22% upside for the years ahead. However, given that the company remains loss-making and I typically look for mature companies to show around 10% upside, I don’t believe the upside that the stock price evaluation returns provide an extremely compelling investment case for flyExclusive, as the risk profile (continued cash burns and operating losses) would command a significantly higher upside to flip from sell to buy. As a result, I am upgrading from sell to hold and note that while I assumed that the company will cover cash shortfalls through debt, issuing shares is also an option as the company has filed a Form S-3 indicating it may sell common stock, preferred stock, debt securities, and other rights to raise up to $250 million.

Conclusion: flyExclusive Cost Rationalization Gains Traction

flyExclusive is significantly cutting costs as the company is transitioning to a fleet that is better able to generate profits. However, even to hold the stock, I believe there are substantial risks as the company remains loss making and I believe turning profitable is a multi-year effort that may lead to shareholder dilution. So, while an EV/Sales assessment at a 2x multiple indicates upside, it is definitely not enough to justify a buy rating.

data.title: Navigating Thematic Investing With THRO
data.description:
By Jay Jacobs

Why Themes Matter

Thematic investing aims to capture long-term structural changes like artificial intelligence (AI) and geopolitical fragmentation, as well as shorter-lived shifts such as the rise of the cost-conscious consumer. Some themes reshape industries over decades, while others make a sharp but brief impact. In both scenarios, timing matters. Knowing when to enter, lean in, or step back can be the difference between capturing a trend or chasing it. (For a deeper look into AI and geopolitical fragmentation, see our Thematic Mid-Year Update.)

THRO: A data-driven thematic approach

One way to access thematic investing is through the iShares U.S. Thematic Rotation Active ETF (THRO). It takes an adaptive, data-driven approach, analyzing millions of news articles, earnings call transcripts, and analyst reports in an effort to pinpoint trends gaining traction with investors and markets. Unlike strategies tied to a single theme, THRO evaluates dozens of themes, aiming to outperform the S&P 500.

Sample data sources

1,000,000+

Financial news articles per year

5,000+

Earrings call transcripts every quarter

1,000+

Source: BlackRock, as of December 31, 2024. The strategies discussed are strictly for illustrative and educational purposes and are not a recommendation, offer, or solicitation to buy or sell any securities or to adopt any investment strategy. There is no guarantee that any strategies discussed will be effective.

Proprietary signals can help guide portfolio positioning. Themes showing strong investor interest, positive price momentum, and attractive valuations may warrant a larger allocation, while those losing relevance or overheating can be dialed down or removed altogether. This dynamic approach can be nimble, rotating into high-conviction opportunities while staying disciplined on risk.

THRO In Action

To see how this process works in action, we look at three of THRO’s current positions: two overweights and one underweight.

TJX Companies (TJX): Tightening the Belt: Down the Consumer Value Chain

With economic uncertainty persisting, consumers are reprioritizing how they spend. A recent McKinsey survey found that most Americans have either adjusted their shopping habits or plan to do so by cutting back or switching to lower-cost brands.1 Recognizing this potential trend, THRO has an overweight exposure to the Tightening the Belt theme.

Not all retailers are equally positioned to benefit. Companies with flexible sourcing models may have an edge over those more dependent on imports. As a leader in off-price retail, TJX Companies (1.4% in THRO)2 sells brand-name goods, often 20%-60% lower than those found in traditional retail, by purchasing excess inventory already located in the U.S.3 This model helps it sidestep supply chain shocks while potentially capitalizing on softening demand in full-price retail.

The Tightening the Belt theme has been a contributor to THRO’s performance, outpacing both Consumer Discretionary and Consumer Staples sectors through May 2025. THRO’s consumer basket reflects an overweight stance relative to the S&P 500’s consumer sectors, tapping into the shift toward value-conscious spending and showing how THRO seeks to turn real-world consumer behavior into investment opportunities.

2025 performance of THR0 thematic consumer exposure overweight vs. consumer sectors of S&P 500 Index

Cisco Systems (CSCO): Capex continued: Tech & infrastructure

Major technology companies are aggressively ramping up capital expenditures, with industry giants expected to spend $305 billion in 2025, largely to advance their AI ambitions.4 These investments extend well beyond chips and servers, flowing into fiber optics, power and cooling systems, and electronic components. THRO seeks to participate in this digital buildout through its overweight in the Capex Continued: Tech & Infrastructure theme.

Cisco Systems (1.3% in THRO)5, a provider of internet networking products and solutions, plays a role in enabling this expansion. As a potential to beneficiary of AI, automation, and reshoring tailwinds, Cisco received $600 million in AI product orders in 3Q25, surpassing its $1 billion full year target a quarter early. Demand from hyperscale cloud providers was a driver, with some accounts increasing orders by more than 100%.6 THRO’s position in Cisco shows how the fund seeks exposure across the broader ecosystem that may go beyond the more obvious names associated with AI.

JPMorgan Chase (JPM): Globally Integrated Service Providers

The impact of regulatory policies and geopolitical tensions has extended beyond supply chains. They’re slowing global capital flows and cross-border deal activity. Investment banking fees fell 6.3% year-over-year in early 2025,7 and a PwC survey found that 30% of deals were paused or revised due to shifting trade policies.8 Even approved deals risk being rerouted to regional players as firms seek more predictable operating environments. Since many U.S. megabanks already have significant domestic exposure, global diversification may be a potential growth driver. In the meantime, the slowdown of global trade could accelerate financial reforms in other regions and reduce the need for U.S.-based services.

JPMorgan Chase (0% in THRO)9, a bellwether for multinational banking and financial services, faces these pressures given its position as a global investment bank. On its 1Q25 earnings call, CEO Jamie Dimon noted that large U.S. banks could be “in the crosshairs” of prolonged trade disputes. JPM’s vast network, moving over $10 trillion daily across 160+ countries in 120 currencies, shows how exposed global players may be to market fragmentation.10 These dynamics support THRO’s underweight position in both Globally Integrated Service Providers theme and JPM.

Conclusion

THRO’s active approach is designed for agility, adjusting exposures as market conditions evolve, leaning into areas of strength, and stepping back from those losing momentum. This discipline seeks to maintain the portfolio aligned with investor priorities while pursuing new opportunities for growth and return.

data.title: Merck: Buy The Weakness
data.description:
Merck (NYSE:MRK) has substantially underperformed the market over the past few years. The company faces the upcoming loss of exclusivity of key drug Keytruda along with a number of expensive acquisitions, such as the recent $10 billion Verona Pharma acquisition. Despite that, the company's financials remain strong as it works to diversify itself and generate strong returns.

Merck Financial Results

Merck had reasonable financial results as it's managed a difficult market.

The company saw a 2% decline in sales but a 1.3% increase in non-GAAP gross margin. That enabled the company's operating earnings to actually go up. However, the company saw a 7% increase in non-GAAP operating expenses along with an increase in tax rates. As a result, the company saw a 5% decline in non-GAAP EPS.

That non-GAAP EPS puts the company at a single-digit P/E indicating its financial strength.

Merck Pipeline

The company has an impressive pipeline with strong drugs in each category.

In Oncology, the company has continued to achieve new indications from Keytruda, while continuing to present data to expand its Oncology portfolio. In infectious diseases, the company is continuing to expand its vaccine treatment, incredibly important vaccines that save lives.

The company's vaccine pipeline is expected to generate more than $5 billion in 2030 sales.

Merck + Verona

The company has announced the acquisition of Verona.

The main target here is "Ohtuvayre" the first novel inhaled COPD maintenance treatment in >20 years. The drug has already been approved, substantially reducing the risk of drug approval that comes with many acquisitions. The drug has had a slow initial start, but peak sales are still forecast to be in the $3-4 billion range.

That'd be very profitable for a $10 billion acquisition.

Merck - Keytruda

The company's Keytruda sales have continued to increase substantially as it finds more and more approval.

The drug continues to be accepted for more and more forms of cancer, with increased updates and support from different indications. Even with the success of Ozempic, the drug has maintained its slot as the best-selling drug, with more than $30 billion in forecast 2025 sales.

The company is working to handle an expected loss of sales, though we expect growing sales mean the drug will remain stronger for longer. The company has tripled its late-phase pipeline since 2021, and sees >$50 billion in mid-2030s opportunity from its pipeline. That's more than enough to make up for the losses from Keytruda.

Merck Shareholder Returns

Overall, the company has updated its guidance on strength, which will support shareholder returns.

The company has bumped revenue guidance by $200 million with flat margins and a $100 million decline at the midpoint in operating expenses. The company also expects average taxes to come down 0.25% at the midpoint. The net result here is that the company's EPS guidance has shifted to be a few cents higher at the midpoint.

That just barely ekes the company into a single digit P/E based on its non-GAAP EPS showing its low valuation.

Those strong earnings give the company substantial cash for shareholder returns despite continued R&D and capital spending. The company's $2.1 billion quarterly dividend is an almost 3.9% dividend yield it can comfortably afford. The company also repurchased $1.3 billion shares in the quarter, ~0.5% of its float.

The company has a $10 billion authorization, enough to repurchase 5% of its outstanding shares. Over the past 12 months, the company has spent $8 billion on dividends and $3.4 billion on share repurchases, both of which it can comfortably afford. As Keytruda hits peak sales until LOE in 3 years, and new drugs come online, immediate-term returns should be strong.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is the uncertain nature of the pharmaceutical industry. The company has a major loss of exclusivities coming up, and it will need to find a way to replace $10s of billions of revenue. The company has an exciting pipeline for that, but there's no guarantee it works as expected, which is worth paying close attention.

Conclusion

Merck's share price has continued to decline, moving it into a single-digit P/E based on its non-GAAP EPS. The company has slightly increased its 2025 revenue guidance and decreased its expense guidance as Keytruda has continued to perform incredibly well. That has enabled the company to maintain its strong dividend and share buybacks.

The company does have the risk of loss of exclusivity, but it has an impressive pipeline. The company's pipeline will start panning out with acquisitions such as Verona and new vaccine approvals. We expect that the company will be able to replace the loss of exclusivity and generate continued long-term shareholder returns.
